retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Fundamentals -- Reverse-Transcription PCR
retrieved_docs.content: The PCR technology has been creatively expanded, and reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR) is one of the most significant advances. Real-time PCR is frequently confused with reverse-transcription PCR, but these are different techniques. In RT-PCR, the DNA amplified is derived from mRNA using reverse-transcriptase enzymes, DNA polymerases expressed by RNA-containing retroviruses, to generate a complementary DNA (cDNA) library. Using primer sequences for genes of interest, traditional PCR methods can be used with the cDNA to study the expression of genes qualitatively. Currently, reverse-transcription PCR is commonly used with real-time PCR, which allows one to quantitatively measure the relative change in gene expression across different samples.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures -- Real-time Polymerase Chain Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Real-time PCR is a widely used method for analyzing small DNA segments, offering advantages such as elimination of post-PCR processing, use of fluorescent dyes or probes, and real-time monitoring of amplicon formation. The primary distinction between real-time and conventional PCR is that real-time PCR allows immediate detection of amplified products during the reaction, rather than after amplification is complete. This process is accomplished through the incorporation of fluorescent molecules, either intercalating dyes or sequence-specific probes, that emit signals proportional to DNA accumulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures -- Reverse Transcription P olymerase Chain Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Reverse transcription PCR (RT-PCR) uses messenger RNA as a template for DNA amplification by reverse transcriptase, often derived from retroviruses, to generate complementary DNA (cDNA). RT-PCR is often combined with conventional PCR to assess specific gene expression qualitatively.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures -- Real-time Polymerase Chain Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Despite these advantages, real-time PCR is more expensive than conventional PCR due to its specialized instrumentation and reagents. Additionally, some fluorescent dyes are not universally compatible with all platforms, owing to hardware limitations, such as excitation and emission wavelength incompatibility or insufficient filter sets. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The main components of PCR are a template, primers, free nucleotide bases, and the DNA polymerase enzyme. The DNA template contains the specific region of interest for amplification, such as DNA extracted from a piece of hair. Primers, or oligonucleotides, are short single strands complementary to each DNA strand of the target region. Both forward and reverse primers are required, one for each complementary strand of DNA. DNA polymerase is the enzyme that carries out DNA replication. Thermostable analogs of DNA polymerase I, such as Taq polymerase, initially found in a bacterium that grows in hot springs, are a common choice due to their resistance to the heating and cooling cycles necessary for PCR. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Several limitations affect the accuracy and reliability of PCR. Extreme sensitivity allows detection of even minimal contamination in DNA or RNA samples, which may produce inaccurate results. Primer design requires precise sequence targeting of specific pathogens or genes. Occasional nonspecific annealing to closely related but unintended sequences can interfere with amplification specificity. Primer-dimer formation, when amplified by DNA polymerase, introduces competition for PCR reagents and reduces overall efficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Fundamentals -- Real-Time PCR
retrieved_docs.content: Improvements in fluorophore development and instrumentation led to thermocyclers that no longer required measurement of only end-product DNA. This process, known as real-time or quantitative PCR (qPCR), has allowed for detecting dsDNA during amplification. The qPCR thermocyclers can excite fluorophores at specific wavelengths, detect their emission with a photodetector, and record the values. The sensitive collection of numerical values during amplification has strongly enhanced quantitative analytical power.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Proteinase K can inhibit PCR by degrading DNA polymerase and other essential proteins if not adequately removed during sample preparation. Other substances that negatively affect PCR include ionic detergents, heparin, spermidine, and hemoglobin. Bromophenol blue and xylene cyanol may also disrupt PCR reactions. To minimize these effects, DNA templates may be purified by dialysis or ethanol precipitation. Additional purification methods include chloroform extraction and chromatography. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Fundamentals -- Real-Time PCR
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, PCR technology was limited to qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis due to the limitations of nucleic acid quantification. At that time, the DNA product was separated by size via agarose gel electrophoresis to verify if the target gene was amplified successfully. Ethidium bromide, a molecule that fluoresces when bound to dsDNA, was used to estimate the amount of DNA by comparing the intensity of separated bands but was not sensitive enough for rigorous quantitative analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: PCR begins with the extraction of a small nucleic acid sample, typically DNA or RNA, into a reaction tube. The process consists of three major phases: denaturation, annealing, and extension. During denaturation, DNA is heated to 95°C to disrupt hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs of the double-stranded molecule. Annealing follows immediately, cooling the denatured DNA to a temperature typically ranging from 55°C to 72°C, allowing primers to bind to their complementary sequences. Annealing is most effective at 55°C to 72°C.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: In PCR, DNA amplification may be monitored using fluorescent dyes that bind to double-stranded DNA or with sequence-specific probes. The amplification process includes a quantification cycle, defined as the number of fractional cycles required for fluorescence to reach a measurable threshold.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is widely employed in basic science and biomedical research. PCR is a laboratory technique to amplify specific DNA segments for various laboratory and clinical applications. Building on the work of Panet and Khorana’s successful amplification of DNA in-vitro, Mullis and coworkers developed PCR in the early 1980s, having been met with a Nobel prize only a decade later.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Polymerase Chain Reaction -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Another potential issue of using PCR is the possibility of primer dimer (PD) formation. PD is a potential by-product and consists of primer molecules that have hybridized with each other due to the strings of complementary bases in the primers. The DNA polymerase amplifies the PD, leading to competition for PCR reagents that could be used to amplify the target sequences. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: PCR offers several advantages in basic and biomedical sciences. This modality has become the gold standard for numerous applications due to its speed, sensitivity, and reliability. Results are typically obtained within a few hours, though extended workflows may take up to 3 days. A minimal input of 1 to 100 ng of DNA or RNA is often sufficient to initiate the reaction, though some highly sensitive assays can use even lower amounts. PCR can amplify 10 6 to 10 9 copies of DNA within a short time. The incorporation of restriction sites at terminal ends enables efficient downstream cloning and expression of amplification products.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Low PCR efficiency requires additional cycles to reach the quantification threshold, resulting in a higher quantification cycle value. When a valid probe-based assay is used, the presence of amplification indicates that the sample contains the target sequence, supporting a positive diagnosis. However, the absence of amplification is not a reliable criterion for classifying a reaction as negative due to Poisson variation.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: After PCR amplification, agarose gel electrophoresis with ethidium bromide staining is typically performed to visualize DNA. The gel is examined under ultraviolet light. To verify result specificity and reduce nonspecific amplification, such as primer dimers, DNA may be transferred to a membrane and probed, as in Southern blot hybridization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 585
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings encompass a broad spectrum of atypical laboratory results, and within this category a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) is identified as a specific child finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” includes specific abnormal results such as a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) and an abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي). These particular findings are considered sub‑categories of the broader group of nonspecific abnormal laboratory results, indicating that when such tests return these outcomes, they represent distinct types of abnormal cytological, histological, immunological, or DNA evidence that require further clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
retrieved_docs.content: subarachnoid hemorrhage, classifying them within a common pathological framework. Integrating surgical, enzymological, microbiological, pathological and virological perspectives therefore supports comprehensive, multidisciplinary diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that improve patient assessment, diagnosis and care across the renal, cardiovascular, obstetric, hematologic, neurologic and gastrointestinal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic endocrine procedures represent a broad category of diagnostic techniques that include الخزعة (biopsy). Within the scope of الخزعة, there are more specific child procedures, such as لطاخة المهبل (vaginal swab), which is a specialized form of biopsy targeting the vaginal mucosa. Recognizing that diagnostic endocrine procedures encompass الخزعة and that الخزعة has لطاخة المهبل as a sub‑procedure helps clinicians understand the hierarchical organization of these diagnostic methods and select the most appropriate technique for endocrine assessments and vaginal tissue sampling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method includes the procedure known as الخزعة (biopsy) as a specific technique for obtaining tissue samples, and diagnostic endocrine procedures are broadly associated with الخزعة, reflecting the use of biopsy in the evaluation of endocrine disorders. This relationship highlights the central role of biopsy both as a method of biospecimen collection and as a key component of diagnostic strategies in endocrine medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 539
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method is defined by the value “الخزعة” (biopsy). The biopsy procedure functions as a method within both diagnostic endocrine procedures and diagnostic procedures; male genital. Within the hierarchy of specimen‑collection techniques, “الخزعة” has a child entity “لطاخة المهبل” (vaginal swab), indicating that vaginal swab is a specific subtype of biopsy‑related collection. Additionally, Transrectal biopsy of prostate is a child of diagnostic procedures; male genital, representing a specialized application of biopsy for prostate evaluation. Together, these relationships illustrate how biopsy serves as a central biospecimen collection method across endocrine and male genital diagnostics, with distinct sub‑procedures such as vaginal swab and transrectal prostate biopsy extending its clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” is a child of immunizations and screening for infectious disease and itself has a child concept of abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي), reflecting the breadth of ancillary testing that may be performed in patients with diabetes or related infectious disease screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2924
retrieved_docs.content: Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy is classified as a combined procedure that includes both an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and a biopsy, indicating that it encompasses the endoscopic examination of the upper GI tract together with the collection of tissue samples for histopathologic analysis. This integrated approach is used to diagnose and evaluate diseases of the upper gastrointestinal system.
